judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all three expectations with the same core findings; they are comparable, with Report 1 slightly more thorough in confirmation detail.

report_1.evidence: `tenant_id` appears only in the function's parameter list and in the SELECT column list — it is never used as a filter predicate. Any authenticated user of any tenant can call `/appointments/search?q=...&status=booked` and receive matching appointments — including the `notes` field and `tenant_id` — for **every tenant in the system**, not just their own.

report_2.evidence: The function accepts a `tenant_id` parameter but never uses it in the hand-rolled SQL ... has no `WHERE tenant_id = ...` clause at all. Every other read path in this codebase goes through `build_appointment_query()` ... any authenticated clinic (tenant) hitting `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=booked` gets back matching appointments ... for **every tenant in the database**

report_1.evidence: does not touch `backend/app/exports.py`, whose `nightly_export()` still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with two arguments. On this branch, every invocation of `nightly_export` ... raises `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`.

report_2.evidence: making `filters` a required positional argument with no default. `backend/app/exports.py` (not touched by this PR) still calls it as `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id).all()` — two arguments only. ... This means `nightly_export()` will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'` the next time it runs

report_1.evidence: Currently **not exploitable** — `status` is typed as `AppointmentStatus(str, Enum)` in `backend/app/schemas.py` with only three fixed values (`booked`/`cancelled`/`completed`), and FastAPI validates the query parameter against that enum before the handler runs, so `status.value` can never contain attacker-controlled characters today.

report_2.evidence: Today this isn't independently exploitable for injection because `status` is typed as the `AppointmentStatus` str-`Enum` (`schemas.py`) on the FastAPI route, so FastAPI/Pydantic rejects any value that isn't `booked`/`cancelled`/`completed` before the function body runs. ... `q`, which correctly uses `:q`
